coredata.dc:title: Construction of AuNPs/Cu,I-CD-based colorimetric sensor: Catalytic oxidation of TBHQ and the catalytic inhibition of HCHO
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qiulan
Yang, Dezhi
Tammina, Sai Kumar
Yang, Yaling
coredata.dc:description: Various research groups have been paying huge attention to tune the metal states in metal–carbon hybrid materials. Herein, a mixed-valence copper-iodine co-doped carbon dots (Cu,I-CDs, Cu2+/Cu+/Cu0) were prepared through a one-step hydrothermal method, which displayed an intrinsic reduction performance under given conditions. Moreover, AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs composite was fabricated using Cu,I-CDs as reductant and stabilizer. Among them, the AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs composite exhibited the highest oxidase- and peroxidase-like activities, which was used for the colorimetric detection of tert-butyl hydroquinone (TBHQ), with the detection limits of 23.45 μg/kg. Interestingly, the catalytic oxidation of TBHQ to oxidized TBHQ (TQ) could be inhibited by formaldehyde (HCHO). Therefore, a colorimetric sensor for HCHO was developed with the detection limit 0.335 mg/L. The catalytic mechanism for TBHQ was investigated by employing scavengers of different reactive species, indicating the significant roles of •O2 − in the catalytic process. Therefore, it is believed that the as-prepared AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs nanozyme has promising potential applications in the fields of biomedicine and food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs
Oxidase mimic
Tert-butyl hydroquinone
Formaldehyde
Colorimetric detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs catalytic activity and colorimetric detection
Possibly catalytic mechanism of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs + TBHQ system
Inhibition of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs + TBHQ system by HCHO

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs (a, b and c); HR-TEM images (d).

XPS patterns (a-g): Survey spectra of Cu,I-CDs and AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs (a); XPS spectra of the Au 4f (b); Cu 2p (c); C 1 s (d); O 1 s (e); N 1 s (f); I 3d (g); Zeta potentials of AuNPs and AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs (h).

OXD-like catalytic properties (a-c): UV absorption spectra the oxidized products of 1 mM TBHQ (red line), OPD (yellow line), TMB (green line), and ABTS (blue line) catalyzed by AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs (50 μg/mL), the corresponding dashed line indicates the TBHQ, OPD, TMB, or ABTS only; Time dependent absorbance at 654 nm measured from the reaction solutions containing 0.01 mM TMB and Cu,I-CDs or AuNPs or AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs (pH = 4.0, room temperature) (b); UV absorption spectra of the oxidation of TBHQ in the presence of Cu,I-CDs or AuNPs or AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs (c); POD-like catalytic properties of three nanozymes for the TMB/H2O2 system (d). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The UV–vis spectra of TBHQ oxidation and HCHO detection in the presence of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs (a); The linear plots for TBHQ and HCHO detection (b); Schematic illustration of TBHQ and HCHO detection (c).

Effect of different scavengers on the UV–vis absorbance of red reaction product (a); The change of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs + TBHQ system absorbance at 492 nm under the presence of different scavengers (1-Blank, 2-DMSO, 3- TBA, 4- Na2C2O4, 5-MT, 6-IPA, 7-AgNO3, 8-TEMPO) (b); Schematic of possible catalytic mechanism involving the AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs (c). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the sensing of TBHQ and HCHO involving the AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs.

head.ce:title: Construction of AuNPs/Cu,I-CD-based colorimetric sensor: Catalytic oxidation of TBHQ and the catalytic inhibition of HCHO

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, Yunnan 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, Yunnan 650500, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, University of Hyderabad, Gachibowli, Telangana 500046, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics, University of Hyderabad, Gachibowli, Telangana 500046, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Various research groups have been paying huge attention to tune the metal states in metal–carbon hybrid materials. Herein, a mixed-valence copper-iodine co-doped carbon dots (Cu,I-CDs, Cu2+/Cu+/Cu0) were prepared through a one-step hydrothermal method, which displayed an intrinsic reduction performance under given conditions. Moreover, AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs composite was fabricated using Cu,I-CDs as reductant and stabilizer. Among them, the AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs composite exhibited the highest oxidase- and peroxidase-like activities, which was used for the colorimetric detection of tert-butyl hydroquinone (TBHQ), with the detection limits of 23.45 μg/kg. Interestingly, the catalytic oxidation of TBHQ to oxidized TBHQ (TQ) could be inhibited by formaldehyde (HCHO). Therefore, a colorimetric sensor for HCHO was developed with the detection limit 0.335 mg/L. The catalytic mechanism for TBHQ was investigated by employing scavengers of different reactive species, indicating the significant roles of •O2 − in the catalytic process. Therefore, it is believed that the as-prepared AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs nanozyme has promising potential applications in the fields of biomedicine and food safety.

ce:section.ce:para: Natural enzymes have attracted widespread attention because of their promising catalytic properties with high specificity and selectivity (Ni, Chen, Jiang, Zhang, Lu, & Chemical, 2019). However, natural enzymes can only exhibit high catalytic activity under mild conditions, and also show low stability and activity of the extreme conditions. But nanozymes are exhibiting intrinsic enzyme-like activity and are also inevitably employed in multiple biomedical applications such as organic pollutant degradation, biosensing and so on. Furthermore, compared to natural enzymes, nanozymes exhibit higher stability with low-cost facile preparation, which is conducive to the widespread utilization. Thus, nanozymes have promising applications in serving as an analogue to natural enzymes. Copper-doped nanozyme, as a relatively new class of nanozymes, shows great potential in the field of catalysis. Until now, a number of copper-doped nanomaterials were found to exhibit intrinsic enzyme-like characteristics, such as peroxidase-like (POD-like) (S. Chen, Li, Huang, & Jia, 2021), superoxide dismutase-like (Aliaga, Andrade-Acuña, López-Alarcón, Sandoval-Acuña, & Speisky, 2013; da Silva, Pougy, da Silva, Lima, & Machado, 2021; Liu, Li, Sun, Xin, & Zhang, 2021), and oxidase-like (OXD-like) activities (Safaei, Bahrami, Pevec, Kozlevčar, & Jagličić, 2017; Tuna, Yildiz, Arabaci, Denli, Delibaş, & Hökelek, 2021; Zhang, Su, Song, Fan, & Xu, 2021; Zhuang, Zhang, Chen, Li, Cao, & Huang, 2019). However, the catalytic activity of a single transition metal is always limited. In addition, only a few works have reported nanomaterials as a reductant and oxidase mimic. This situation clearly shows that it is very important to synthesize stable and efficient materials with dual functions of reducing agent and oxidase (Scheme 1 ).

Compared with individual enzyme mimics, complexes show higher catalytic activity, better stability and durability (Hoang, Lin, Le, Le, Huynh, & Tsai, 2021; Zhao, Chu, Xiao, & Chen, 2021). For example, Aarti et al. prepared gold-boron nitride nanosheets (Au-BNNs) and silver-boron nitride nanosheets (Ag-BNNs) with peroxidase-like activity by fabricating the compound of gold (Au) and silver (Ag) nanoparticles onto BNNs (Deshmukh, Aloui, & Kim, 2020). Zhang et al. reported PP-based Ag-Cu composite with good catalytic efficiency and stability (Zhang, Shen, Guo, Yan, Chen, Hu, et al., 2021). Anxin et al. prepared Au/Cu2O heterostructures with promising POD catalytic activity compared with bare Cu2O (Jiao, Xu, Tian, Cui, Liu, & Chen, 2021). Hence, the advantage of these methods is that assembling Au improves the catalytic activity of nanozymes.

In general, tert-butylhydroquinone (TBHQ) is used as a common food additive to prevent oxidation and deterioration of food. Normal levels are linked to avert oxidative deterioration, but high TBHQ levels may lead to the development of malformation and cancer (Balram, Lian, Sebastian, & Rasana, 2021). Based on this situation, the addition of TBHQ has been strictly regulated (P. Li, Yang, Lee, Huang, Wang, & Li, 2021). At the same time, formaldehyde (HCHO) is a food pollutant in every-day life as it is a side-product of brewing processes (Akshath & Bhatt, 2018). Even low levels of HCHO can cause serious harm to the human health including nasal tumors (Ahmad, Park, Lee, Kim, Kim, Jurng, et al., 2021). Therefore, it is essential to make effective detection methods to monitor the TBHQ and HCHO levels in food. Current methods for detecting the HCHO includes instrument methods (e.g. chromatography, fluorescence spectrophotometry, etc.) and derivatization methods (e.g. acetylacetone and 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine, etc.) (Jiang, Wang, Wang, Liu, You, Lv, et al., 2020). However, there are few reports on detection of TBHQ and HCHO through the inhibition of nanozymes.

Herein, Cu,I-CDs with a mixed-valence Cu element (Cu2++Cu++Cu0) as reductant and oxidase mimic was prepared. The effect of mole ratio of elements (Cu:I) for the reduction and oxidase-like activity of Cu,I-CDs were assessed by cytochrome c (Cyt c) and Fe(III). AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs was synthesized using Cu,I-CDs as reductant and stabilizer, which has stronger oxidase-like activity than Cu,I-CDs and AuNPs alone. The AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs can catalyze the oxidation of TBHQ, and HCHO hamper the OXD-like activity of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs through obstructing the superoxide radical (•O2 −) generation and electron transfer processes. Therefore, a colorimetric on–off switch has been developed for TBHQ and HCHO detection in real samples. Ultimately, a reasonable mechanism was proposed to well explain the catalytic process of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs for TBHQ and the inhibitory effect of HCHO on this catalytic system.

The Cu,I-CDs nanozyme was synthesized using one-step hydrothermal method. In a typical procedure, CuCl2 and 3-iodo-l-tyrosine (the mole ratio was 5:4) were mixed in a teflon-lined autoclave, which was heated for 8 h at 180°C. After cooling down naturally to room temperature, the product was filtered through the 0.22 μm membrane. The filtrate was stored at 4 °C for further experiments.

Firstly, 80 μL of 1% HAuCl4 aqueous and 40 mL deionized water were mixed in a beaker and stirred vigorously in magnetic stirrer. Secondly, aqueous Cu,I-CDs (1.948 mg/mL, 200 μL) was added and stirred vigorously at room temperature for 30 min to obtain a purple solution. Finally, the color of the solution immediately changed to purple, and AuNPs/Cu, I-CDs were obtained.

The chemical valence states of the as-prepared samples were examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on a Thermo Scientific ESCALab 250Xi equipped with 200 W of monochromatic Al Kα radiation. The morphology and microstructure of samples were obtained by TecnaiG2 TF30 Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) operating at 200 kV. Zeta potential analysis of materials was performed on a Zeta sizer-Nano ZSZEN3600 apparatus (Malvern Instruments, UK). The detection of UV–vis spectra was conducted by a dual-beam UV spectrophotometer (TU-1900, China). The fluorescence spectra of the samples were detected using an Agilent Technologies' CaryEclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The OXD-like activity of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs composite was evaluated by catalyzing the chromogenic substrates (3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), o-phenylenediamine (OPD), 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), and TBHQ) in the absence of H2O2. The total volume was 4 mL, which contains 100 μL of the catalyst (0.1 mg/mL, i.e. AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs or Cu,I-CDs or AuNPs), 400 μL of the substrate (5 mM) and 3.5 mL of deionized water. The POD-like activity was assessed by the same method with the participation of H2O2.

Typical Michaelis − Menten curves were obtained for the AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs or Cu,I-CDs with TBHQ as a substrate in a certain range of concentrations (0.125–0.8 mM). In a typical reaction, AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs or Cu,I-CDs (100 μL, 0.1 mg/mL) were mixed with different concentration of TBHQ. The total volume of the solution is 4 mL, and the change in absorbance within 300 s is continuously detected. The kinetic parameters (K m and V max) were calculated from the Lineweaver-Burk plot:
1 v
=
K m
V
max
×
1
S
+
1
V
max
where v is the initial velocity, V max represents the maximal reaction velocity, K m is Michaelis constant and [S] is the substrate (TBHQ) concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs catalytic activity and colorimetric detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to identify the generation of reactive oxygen radicals, under the same conditions, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) and methanol (MT), 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidinooxy (TEMPO), isopropanol (IPA), AgNO3, tert-butanol (TBA), were added into 400 μL 5 mM TBHQ solutions dispersed with 100 μL AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs to trap ∙O2 −, hydroxyl radical (∙OH), electronic (e−) and hole (h+), respectively. The reduction in absorption of TBHQ oxidation caused by the scavenger was measured spectrophotometrically at 492 nm.

Detection of TBHQ: Solutions containing different TBHQ concentrations (0.35–200 mg/kg) were added to an aqueous solution containing AuNPs/ Cu,I-CDs (100 μL, 0.1 mg/mL) and subsequently incubated at room temperature for 10 min followed by monitoring the change in absorbance at the given wavelength.

Detection of HCHO: Different concentrations (0.67–26.67 mg/L) of HCHO were added to Na2CO3-NaHCO3 buffer (pH = 8) containing AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs + TBHQ system, and then the solution was incubated 12 min at room temperature.

Cu,I-CDs can not only act as reducing agents ( Fig. S1
a and b) but also act as a stabilizer for the preparation of AuNPs. Fig. S2
a shows the optical absorption properties of the prepared samples. With the introduction of AuNPs, there was a change in color (transformed to purple), and absorption of Cu,I-CDs were observed in AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs. A typical absorption peak of AuNPs (NaBH4 as reducing agent) was observed at 530 nm (Stuti Bhagat, Srikanth Vallabani, Shutthanandan, Bowden, Karakoti, & Singh, 2018). However, the absorption peak of the AuNPs presented with the participation of Cu,I-CDs show a red-shift of 17 nm, which could be ascribed to the interaction between Cu,I-CDs and AuNPs. These changes implied the formation of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs successfully. The nanocomposites showed stable fluorescence at 590 nm with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, and the absorption wavelength was observed at 550 nm ( Fig. S2
b).

The morphology features of AuNPs, Cu,I-CDs, and AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs was tested by TEM and high-resolution TEM (Fig. 1 (a–d)). The average diameters of Cu,I-CDs ( Fig. S3
a) and AuNPs ( Fig. S3
b) were ∼ 3 and 34 nm, respectively. However, most of the obtained AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs exhibited a circular-like structure with a size of 50–65 nm (Fig. 1c). This might be attributed to the aggregation of AuNPs. HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1d) displayed the lattice fringes with a separation of 0.24 nm, which is in good agreement with the (111) crystal plane of Au (Jeong, Ikoma, Yu, Bae, Lee, Han, et al., 2021; Lei, Jiao, Weiqing, Hongye, Yan, Wenling, et al., 2019).

XPS analysis was carried out to demonstrate the formation of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs composite and characterize the chemical composition, binding energy and oxidation state of the elements present in the AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs and Cu,I-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, XPS survey spectrum verified the typical signals of Cu,I-CDs (Cu, I, C, N, and O) and AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs (Au, Cu, I, C, N, and O). Apparently, with the information of AuNPs, the Cu LMM peak intensity decreased but the Cu 2p peak intensity increased ( Fig. S4
a and b), which is consistent with the change of oxidation state of Cu (Sang, Yun, Heo, Siah, & Gordon, 2014). Fig. 2b displays the XPS spectra of Au 4f, which reveals the successful reduction of Au3+ to Au0. Two peaks at 84.3 eV (Au 4f7/2) and 88.1 eV (Au 4f5/2) may be attributed to the XPS peaks for Au0 (Zhang, Ji, Wang, Shao, & Huang, 2020). Further, the Cu 2p spectrum (Fig. 2c) was deconvoluted into four peaks at 943.3, 952.7, 956.7, and 932.7 eV, respectively, which could be attributed to Cu2+ 2p3/2, Cu2+ 2p1/2, Cu+ 2p1/2, and Cu+ 2p3/2 of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs composite (Zhao, Zhang, Zhao, Li, Li, Wen, et al., 2015). Two peaks at 563.2 eV and 572.3 eV confirmed the presence of Cu+ and Cu0. The C 1 s spectra (Fig. 2d) was deconvoluted into three components with binding energies at 284.8, 286.2, and 288.2 eV corresponding to CC, CO/CN, and CO groups, respectively (Luo, Ma, Jiao, Xing, Zhang, Zhou, et al., 2017). For the XPS spectra of O 1 s (Fig. 2e), the binding energies at 531.8 and 533.0 eV are assigned to CO and C-OH bonds. The N 1 s spectra (Fig. 2f) could be deconvoluted into two peaks with binding energies of 399.8 eV and 401.8 eV, which were ascribed to the CN and NH respectively (Zi, Yu, Tong, Zhao, Chao, Lu, et al., 2015). Two peaks in high-resolution I 3d spectrum (Fig. 2g) situated at 619.0 eV and 632.0 eV may belong to I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2. The I 3d XPS reveals four fitting peaks at 619.0 eV and 628.0 eV, 620.8 eV and 632.0 eV, corresponding to C-I, and N-I, respectively (Ning, Lin, Tong, Zhang, & Wang, 2016).

Zeta potential was recorded to observe the surface charge of materials, which is a significant sign of colloidal stability. The zeta potentials of AuNPs and AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs were showed in Fig. 2h, where AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs and AuNPs exhibited zeta potential values of 24.9 mV and 17.5 mV, respectively. This indicated that colloidal AuNPs showed moderate stability than AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs, and even displayed good dispersion in the presence of Cu,I-CDs.

The OXD-like activity of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs was evaluated with several typical chromogenic substrates, including TMB, ABTS, OPD and TBHQ. As displayed in Fig. 3 a, AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs can catalyze the oxidation of all three colorless substrates to blue, bluish-green, yellow, and red colored products with maximum absorbance at 654, 415, 447 (Luo, Huang, Liu, Zhang, & Wang, 2019) and 492 nm in the absence of H2O2 (Garcia-Molina, Berna, García-Ruiz, Moreno, Martinez, et al., 2014), respectively. Those results confirmed that the AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs possesses intrinsic OXD-like activity. The OXD-like activities of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs, AuNPs, and Cu,I-CDs were investigated by the oxidation of TMB (Fig. 3b) and TBHQ (Fig. 3c). Both AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs and Cu,I-CDs demonstrated the obvious OXD-like activity, and AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs exhibited the highest catalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 3b, compared with Cu,I-CDs, it was apparent that the reaction rates were enhanced while coupling Cu,I-CDs with AuNPs. Consequently, the AuNPs effectively promoted the catalytic performance of Cu,I-CDs. Usually, the colorimetric assay is based on the oxidation reaction of TBHQ to form a red oxidation product (TQ) and which exhibits a maximum absorbance at 492 nm (Garcia-Molina et al., 2014). Interestingly, in presence of either Cu,I-CDs or Au NPs, only a slight or no color change was observed, but in the presence of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs significant color change was observed from colorless to red (shown in Fig. 3c).

The POD-like catalytic activity of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs, AuNPs, and Cu,I-CDs were also assessed via the oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 3d, these materials could catalyze the oxidation of TMB, indicating that AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs, AuNPs, and Cu-ICDs can perform the POD-like activity. But particularly, the AuNPs significantly improved the POD-like activity of Cu,I-CDs. The result might be due to the key role of AuNPs in the decomposition of H2O2, which is conducive to the ∙OH generation (Bhagat, Vallabani, Shutthanandan, Bowden, Singh, & Science, 2017).

The kinetic assay of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs and Cu,I-CDs were conducted by measuring the initial rate of TBHQ at different concentrations. To quantify the catalytic efficiency of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs ( Fig. S5
a and b) and Cu-ICDs ( Fig. S5
c and d), Michaelis − Menten constant (Km ) and maximum initial velocity (V max) were calculated by using double reciprocal plots. As shown in Fig. S5 , the kinetics of TBHQ as substrate conform to the typical Michaelis − Menten curves. The Km values of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs with TBHQ are lower than that of the Cu-ICDs (Table S1), which suggested a higher binding affinity of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs to TBHQ. Furthermore, the compound of AuNPs in oxidation processes showed a positive effect.

To demonstrate the robustness of the detection assay of TBHQ (TBHQ: 6 mg/kg), the influence of the potential coexistence on the assay was investigated using food additives (amino acids, sugar and other antioxidants) ( Fig. S6
c and d). All ions were allowed at low concentrations of 6 g/kg, except for AA (3.42 g/kg). However, no food additives could affect the determination of TBHQ ( Fig. S6
c). Moreover, the influence of pH and temperature for the catalytic system were examined. The results showed that the optimum pH is 7 ( Fig. S6
e). Although the optimum temperature was found to be 60 ℃ ( Fig. S6
f), but a significantly high catalytic activity was observed even at lower temperatures (40–50 ℃).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possibly catalytic mechanism of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs + TBHQ system
ce:section.ce:para:
The active species trapping experiments were conducted to survey the catalytic oxidation mechanism. To determine the principal active radicals, the IPA and AgNO3 as e− scavengers, TBA and DMSO as •OH scavengers, Na2C2O4 and MT as h+ scavengers, TEMPO as •O2 − scavengers were used respectively. Fig. 5
a and b shows the scavengers influence on absorbance and changes in absorbance concerning time. In the presence of TEMPO, the absorbance was decreased rapidly when compared with the reaction system without TEMPO, indicating a major contribution of •O2 − during the catalysis of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs. However, a slightly decrease in the absorbance of the oxidation system was observed even in the presence of other free radical scavengers (Fig. 5 b). These results indicated that all these active species produced were responsible for the oxidation of TBHQ. Nevertheless, •OH, e− and h+ were not the main reactive species in this oxidation process.

Fig. S4
c shows the XPS spectra of Cu 2p of Cu,I-CDs. The Cu 2p peaks at around 932.5 eV and 953.1 eV could be attributed to the signal Cu2+ and Cu+. The Cu 2p peaks at 932.5 eV and 953.1 eV were due to the signals of Cu2+ and Cu+. AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs is displaying two peaks at 943.5 and 952.7 eV (Fig. 1c), but these two peaks were not found in the Cu,I-CDs. This phenomenon verified that the change in chemical states of Cu (Ott, Bhargava, & O’Mullane, 2012; Singh, Neti, Sinha, & Srivastava, 2007; Pang et al., 2012):
(1) 2Au3+ + 3Cu → 3Cu2+ + 2Au
(2) 2Au3+ + 6Cu+ + 3H2O → 6Cu2+ + 2Au + 6H+ +3∙O2 −

Considering the above analysis results of XPS and previous reports (Stuti Bhagat, Srikanth Vallabani, Shutthanandan, Bowden, Karakoti, & Singh, 2018; He, Sun, Wu, Di, & Fei, 2021), it can be deduced that the reaction paths and mechanism over AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs could take place as follows (Fig. 5 c and Eqs. (3)–(7):
(3) Cu+ + O2 → Cu2+ + ∙O2 −
(4) Cu2+ + ∙O2 − + 2H+ → Cu+ + H2O2
(5) AuNPs + H2O2 → ∙OH + ∙OH
(6) Cu+ + ∙OH → Cu2+ + OH−
(7) Cu2+ + OH− + e− → Cu+ + ∙O2 − + H+

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs + TBHQ system by HCHO
ce:section.ce:para:
The AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs + TBHQ system oxidized red product was reduced to the colorless TBHQ in the presence of HCHO. To further verify the possibility of practical applications, the possible interferences of samples were carried out under the optimal experimental conditions, such as metal ions, CH3OH, CH3CHO and C6H5OH ( Fig. S7
c). Interestingly, compared to the reaction system without the participation of HCHO, the absorbance value of the AuNPs/Cu, I-CDs/TBHQ + HCHO system displayed an obvious decrease, indicating the possibility of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs + TBHQ system for the detection of HCHO. Therefore, different concentrations of HCHO were mixed with the AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs oxidation system and the absorption changes were measured after incubated for 20 min. As shown in Fig. 5 a and 5b (yellow line), the absorbance of the red oxidized product decreased with increasing concentration of HCHO, and a good linear relationship was established in the range of 0.67–26.67 mg/L at 492 nm with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.335 mg/L (signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio of 3). Moreover, interference studies showed that the presence of high concentrations of interfering molecules can only produce negligible effects ( Fig. S7
c). Table S4 clearly revealed that the proposed colorimetric assay for HCHO possesses satisfactory recovery efficiencies (99.59–103.38%) in the beer and liquor. The results indicated that the method exhibited excellent selectivity for the HCHO determination. Furthermore, compared with other reported HCHO detection methods, the linear range and detection limit of this method are equal or even better than that of other methods (Table S5).

It can be inferred that the inhibition of HCHO for AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs + TBHQ system was built on the consumption of OH− and ∙OH (Chen, Wang, Chen, Wang, Qin, Zhang, et al., 2020; Li, Cui, Chen, Dong, Chu, Sheng, et al., 2020), which effectively inhibited the electronic transfer between AuNPs and Cu2+/Cu+ (Fig. 5 c).
(8) HCHO + 3OH− → HCOO− + H2O + 2e−
(9) HCHO + 2∙OH → CO2 + 2H+ + H2O

A one-step hydrothermal method was used to prepare Cu,I-CDs nanozyme. The obtained Cu, I-CDs showed excellent reduction performance in the preparation of AuNPs nanozymes. Interestingly, AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs composite exhibited OXD-like activity with lower Km and higher Vmax for TBHQ substrate compared to Cu,I-CDs alone. Based on the promising OXD-like activity of AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs, a rapid, low-cost and highly sensitive colorimetric assay for TBHQ measurement was established. Furthermore, HCHO could consume the generated OH− and ·OH in the AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs system. Thus, TBHQ oxidation by AuNPs/Cu,I-CDs was further exploited for the colorimetric sensor of HCHO. Moreover, the two sensors were developed and successfully used to detect the TBHQ and HCHO in actual samples. The detection results met their corresponding composition descriptions or specifications, which showed that the practical application of these methods in the detection of TBHQ and HCHO has good prospects.
Qiulan Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Dezhi Yang: Validation, Software. Sai Kumar Tammina: Investigation, Validation. Yaling Yang: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was greatly supported by the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (grant numbers 2019P20173118001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.131438.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Ahmad et al., 2021
